data.title: Imperial Brands: Neutral Rated As Valuation Is At Where It Should Be
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a hold rating for Imperial Brands (OTCQX:IMBBY) as the 1H25 results, while encouraging on execution, have areas that I find concerning. Yes, pricing remains strong, market share is up in key markets, and NGP losses are narrowing. But revenue momentum in NGP is mixed, market share gains are uneven, and tobacco volumes continue to decline. With the stock trading at ~8.5x forward earnings—roughly in line with its 10-year average—I don’t think there’s enough upside to get excited here.

Company Description

IMBBY is a UK-based tobacco company that is the fourth-largest cigarette manufacturer internationally. Its legacy cigarette brands include Davidoff, Gauloises, and Winston. The new strategy IMBBY deploys today is to push into the next-generation products (NGP) space, particularly with its blu e-vapor brand. IMBBY's core business is split into Tobacco and NGP, which includes manufacturing, marketing, and sales of both traditional and new products, and Distribution, which runs through Logista Integral (IMBBY owns just over half of Logista), which is publicly traded in Spain and handles both IMBBY and third-party products across Europe.

1H25 Earnings Review

IMBBY 1H25 results largely met expectations, with reported revenue coming in at GBP14.6 billion, driven by core business (tobacco and NGP) net revenue growth of 3.2% y/y in constant currency (CC) to GBP3.66 billion. Pricing was the key driver here, while volume and FX were headwinds. Within tobacco, Europe saw 1.1% net revenue growth despite a 2.2% volume decline; the Americas saw 4.9% revenue growth due to a very strong 10.1% price/mix contribution; and the AAACE region saw 2.5% growth driven mostly by price/mix. As for NGP, it grew 15.4% y/y CC to GBP160 million. Europe led the growth with 17.2% y/y, while the Americas grew 60% thanks to Zone’s (this is IMBBY’s modern oral nicotine pouch) momentum. AAACE was weaker, down 58.3% due to regulatory issues and shipment timing. As for Logista, the distribution arm, it saw 5.6% gross profit y/y growth CC to GBP764 million, but EBIT was flat y/y at GBP166 million.

At the group level, adj. EBIT grew 1.8% to GBP1.65 billion, and adj. EPS grew 6% to 123.9 pence. Overall, I thought the 1H25 results reinforce the same narrative that IMBBY continues to offset volume declines with pricing gains, gradually scaling NGP and keeping profitability intact.

Positive Operational Highlights and Strategic Execution

My positive takeaway from the 1H25 results is beyond the headline numbers noted above. The real positive takeaway, I think, is the solid progress in execution, particularly in its core tobacco business. The biggest win was market share—IMBBY grew aggregate share across its five priority markets (US, Germany, UK, Spain, and Australia) by 6 bps. In particular, Germany was a highlight with a 65 bps gain, marking this the first material share gain there in over a decade. This share gain is very encouraging because it shows that the management strategy of more targeted brand-building and reinvestment in the sales force is working. Aside from validating that the strategy is working, the most important takeaway here is this gives IMBBY more pricing power (to reiterate, tobacco price/mix grew 5.9% y/y and was the key driver behind the 2.7% revenue growth in constant currency). In my view, because of the brand-building work IMBBY has been doing over the past few years, it has helped to build up a more loyal customer base, and this is the holy grail to set up for more price increases over the long term.

NGP also showed momentum. Segment revenue grew 15.4% y/y CC and adj. EBIT losses narrowed by 14% y/y to GBP43 million, and this is despite investments ramping up. In the US, Zone now has a 2.6% national share and >5% in its own retail footprint and has >70,000 store placements secured in just a year. This is a very impressive start, which instills confidence that this growth momentum could continue to go on, as there is a strong secular tailwind in modern oral nicotine pouch demand. Using the execution success seen in heated tobacco, where Pulze 2.0 is gaining traction in Italy, reaching 3.3% share (share up 90 bps), I am inclined to think IMBBY has a product that fits the market, and given that NGP has been a key growth driver for many tobacco companies, I believe IMBBY is making the right decision here.

Challenges and Areas to Monitor

Now, while I like the execution, I have reasons to believe now is not the time to invest in the stock. IMBBY’s NGP segment still needs to prove its worth. Yes, revenue grew 15.4% y/y in CC, losses narrowed meaningfully, and the market share gains are encouraging, but revenue growth did not match up to the hype. Europe NGP sales growth slowed 900 bps from 22% in 2H24; the Americas NGP revenue growth of 60% y/y slowed dramatically from 101% in 2H24; and the AAACE region saw a sharp 58.3% y/y revenue decline to just GBP5 million (due to regulatory shifts in Poland, shipment timing issues, and an exit from the Czech Republic). The latter is worrisome because it shows that despite all the product innovation and brand building, sales can still be down sharply and there is still a lot of demand volatility. The regulatory shift is outside of IMBBY’s control and could hit every possible region, and the exit from the Czech Republic shows that even with strong secular tailwinds, there just may not be enough demand yet.

On the combustible tobacco side, I really liked that IMBBY gained market share across its five priority markets overall, but this does not take away my attention from the fact that both Spain and the UK posted share losses—down 90 bps and 70 bps, respectively. Management was upfront about this being a “deliberate pricing action,” in that they chose to prioritize value over volume. That makes sense from a brand standpoint, but it also shows how vulnerable IMBBY's market share position is. As a sin, in markets facing inflationary or regulatory pressure (forced to raise prices due to duty tariffs), IMBBY could continue to see market share loss if it wants to protect its brand’s value. As such, I am not ready to be very bullish about IMBBY's share gains in its five markets just yet until I see that it is able to sustain that share level.

Taking a step back, I am also wary and worried about the secular volume decline theme. Volumes fell 3.2% y/y in 1H25, and it's especially tough in developed markets. The US remains particularly tough due to increasing illicit vape use and weak consumer spending, and management acknowledged there haven’t been any meaningful improvements in both issues. Australia's volumes also fell sharply due to high excise taxes and illegal trade. Even the UK saw a steep volume decline, driven by duty hikes. As such, while IMBBY group revenue is still holding up well thanks to pricing, this volume drag narrative will remain as a structural issue and I think will act as a major overhang on the stock’s valuation for a very long time.

Finally, the CEO transition adds another layer of uncertainty. Stefan Bomhard will step down on October 1 after a five-year tenure, and this is surprising, especially since he recently announced the 2030 strategy. The current CFO, Lukas Paravicini, will take over as CEO, with Strategy Chief Murray McGowan moving into the CFO role. As much as I think these two personnel are solid enough to run the show, leadership transitions always carry some risk in terms of execution and culture fit.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, I don’t think it screams cheap enough to justify “ignoring” the concerns I have and giving a buy rating. It trades at 8.5x forward PE, which is the historical average for the past 10 years. This seems to be a fair multiple given the solid execution it has demonstrated, offset by the concerns I have—NGP revenue growth, market share gains sustainably, and structural headwinds in tobacco volume. Its closest peer—British American Tobacco (BATS)—is also trading at a similar range, at 10x forward PE, which I think the premium is fair since BATS is expected to grow revenue faster than IMBBY (mid-single-digit 2-year CAGR vs. IMBBY at low-single-digit).

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for IMBBY. Execution is clearly improving, and management deserves credit for scaling new growth areas. But in my view, the stock is fairly valued, trading at a 10-year average multiple, and lacks a near-term catalyst for rerating. Unless NGP growth reaccelerates or volumes start to stabilize, it’s hard to argue for anything more than a market-perform view. I’ll revisit if momentum improves, but for now, the risk/reward looks balanced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 221: $22,100 Allocated, $2,225.40 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was another rough week for the markets as the S&P 500 closed down -1.85% and the Nasdaq fell -1.51%. The weakness started with Moody's downgrading U.S. debt from Aaa to Aa1 due to large fiscal deficits, which led to a spike in treasury yields. Treasury yields spiked again after President Trump's proposed tax-cut bill gained more steam and reports surfaced that it could add $3 trillion to the deficit over the next decade. The U.S. and China reached an agreement to slash tariffs for 90 days, which boosted morale across the markets. We will see what happens when the tax-cut bill goes through the Senate and what implications this will have on businesses and the deficit. We have a FOMC meeting approaching starting on June 18th, and CME Group is projecting that there is a 97.8% chance that rates remain unchanged. I think the Fed will wait and see what the next set of CPI and PPI numbers look like, as well as any movement on trade deals, before starting to cut again. While I believe the Fed should have already started their cutting cycle due to unemployment continuing to increase, I don't think they're going to start cutting until September at this point. There is a lot of uncertainty, and the tax-bill may add too much fuel to the fire, but the driving factor that not enough people are discussing will likely be unemployment. We need unemployment to decline, and to do that, and we need borrowing costs to decline so more entrepreneurs can start businesses and existing businesses are incentivized to expand. I am going to take this opportunity to continue adding too many positions across the portfolio, as I still believe the market will do very well in the 2nd half of the year and that there are many opportunities to allocate capital.

The market retraced this week, especially after Moody's decreased the rating on the U.S. debt. After allocating $22,100 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 221 at $27,300.97. This was a weekly decline of -2.11% (-$440.62), taking the account's profitability to $5,200.97, which is a return on invested capital of 23.53%. This week, I added to my positions in the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and BP p.l.c. (BP). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $19.69 of dividend income in week 221. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $11.64 (0.53%) to $2,225.40. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,059.77 of income since inception, which is 18.37% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The monthly dividend income continues to increase as I am constantly allocating capital toward the portfolio. The dividend snowball continues to grow, and every dollar I have generated in dividend income has been reinvested back into the underlying positions. It's not even June, and I am getting close to reaching $2,250 of dividend income. I am looking forward to evaluating my initial 2025 projections, as I think that I will be exceeding $2,500 of forward income by the time 2025 ends. I am looking forward to seeing how the portfolio progresses because there are many companies that should do well when the rate-cutting cycle begins.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This is not a portfolio focused on capital appreciation, and I am very happy with the performance. The goal has been to build an income-producing portfolio from the ground up, brick by brick. I started the portfolio with $100 and have added $100 each week while documenting every dividend generated and where every dollar has been allocated. My investment parameters have been to not exceed a 5% portfolio weighting in a single position or 20% for a sector. The portfolio goals are to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation is a secondary goal that, I believe, will be achieved. When I look at the chart below, my investment goals are being met as the downside has never been exaggerated too far to the downside while the portfolio has been able to navigate every macroeconomic environment and geopolitical headwind that has been thrown at it. I believe the portfolio will continue doing well, and I am excited to continue allocating capital toward it.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $671.38 (30.17%)
  • Equities $550.48 (24.74%)
  • CEFs $391.65 (17.60%)
  • REITs $368.53 (16.56%)
  • BDCs $243.36 (10.94%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 21 weeks in 2025, $881.39 of dividend income was produced, which is 54.30% of the total income from 2024. There were 287 positions that paid a dividend, which is 40.48% of the dividends generated in 2024.

It's incredible that 21 weeks have passed, and the dividend income being generated continues to impress me. This week, there was $19.69 of dividend income generated, which took my weekly average to $41.97. This represents a 34.46% increase, as an additional $10.76 of dividend income is generated on a weekly basis compared to last year. When I compare the 2 charts below, the weekly income looks completely different, and I have already had 2 weeks exceed $100 of income generated, while I only exceeded $80 once in the first 39 weeks of 2024. I think it's entirely possible that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will exceed $2,500 in forward dividend income by the end of 2025, and $125 will exceed the new high on weekly income.

We're heading into the last week of the May, and I have already generated $120.52 of dividend income this month. I am excited to see where the monthly income finishes, as I have already exceeded what was produced in May 2024. The progression is very interesting, as I have averaged a 58.57% YoY increase in monthly dividend income across the first 4 months of 2025. I want to see how close I can get to generate $200 of monthly dividend income in May, as I feel it's going to happen sooner or later. Once June is over, I will make some new predictions, as I feel I may be able to get to $225 or $250 of monthly dividend income before the end of the year.

British American Tobacco (BTI) no longer generates 1 share from its dividends, which means there are 43 positions accomplishing this goal. The new shares that are produced from these positions are expected to generate $230.08 in additional dividend income. I am continuing to work on several positions and see a lot of value in many companies within the portfolio. I still believe that the goal of having at least 50 positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends before the end of the year is possible to reach, and I plan on focusing a portion of the weekly capital toward these positions. Over the next several years, I am hoping that the table below is lopsided and there are way more companies generating at least 1 share from the income they produce than positions that aren't.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

The gap between ETFs and REITs is expanding as REITs retreat closer to 18% of the portfolio. I am glad neither of these sectors is pushing against the 20% threshold, and while I added to JEPI this week, I am trying not to allocate capital toward ETFs as much as other sectors. Individual equities now represent 39.20% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 24.74% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.80% of the portfolio and generate 75.26% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

It's incredible that Altria Group (MO) continues to remain above 4.5% of the portfolio and increased to 4.77% of the portfolio this week. I haven't added to MO since February 2024, and its share price has exceeded so much that it's overshadowing the rest of the positions. I am trying to get the top-4 positions to represent less of the portfolio, and my goal is to have all of them representing less than 3.5% of the portfolio. I think that the top-20 positions are going to change a bit over the next several months, as I see a bunch of opportunities I am looking to allocate capital toward. The long-term goal

The top-10 positions changed this week as SL Green Realty (SLG) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) were replaced by JEPI and BTI. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,410.92, and they finished the week with a value of $8,271.11. This is an ROI of $1,860.19 (29.02%). There has been $1,063.70 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 16.59% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $619.25 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.66%. These positions represent 30.30% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.83% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 221 Additions

In week 221, I added to my positions in JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF, Blue Owl Capital Corporation, and BP p.l.c.

JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF

  • I recently wrote an article discussing my investment thesis on JEPI (can be read here)
  • I wanted to add another share of JEPI heading into NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) earnings as I feel it has a good chance to lift the market and many of the underlying holdings in JEPI higher
  • JEPI has generated 59 monthly distributions since inception and paid investors $24.14, which is 48.27% of its IPO price
  • JEPI continues to deliver reliable high single-digit income, and I plan on compounding it for years to come
  • I like JEPI's portfolio, especially since they take a conservative approach compared to other high-income funds

BP p.l.c.

  • Oil is back over $60, and BP can still do well in this environment, unlike smaller upstream producers
  • I think that BP under $30 is a great opportunity, and it's currently yielding over 6.5%
  • The world is going to need more energy due to the increasing population and the number of data centers getting spun up.
  • I think that BP is a solid long-term income play that is trading at a discount to where it will be in the future

Blue Owl Capital Corporation

  • I had some capital left over this week, so I added to OBDC
  • I think OBDC will do well in the back half of 2025 because when the Fed starts cutting rates, it should see increased originations across its loan book
  • OBDC yields over 10%, and I want to lock in those yields while I still can

Week 222 Game Plan

Next week I am planning on adding to NNN REIT (NNN), the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), and the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG).

Conclusion

So far, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has gone from allocating $100 and generating $7.44 in forward dividend income in week 1 to having allocated $22,100 and generating $2,225.40 in forward dividend income in week 221. I am generating a 10.07% yield on cost and an 8.15% portfolio yield as I am up 23.53% on my initial capital invested. My long-term strategy of building an income-producing portfolio is working, as the portfolio has mitigated downside risk well and continues to generate recurring income. I am excited to see how things progress in 2025 as the dividend snowball continues to grow each week. I am projected to generate $185.45 in monthly dividend income, and I believe that by the end of 2025, I will be exceeding $200 per week on average. I look forward to interacting with everyone in the comment section, so please leave any suggestions, questions, or feedback that you have.

data.title: FSCO: Top-Tier CEF That Delivers Capital Appreciation And Reliable Income
data.description:
Overview

Credit investments continue to grow in popularity due to their ability to serve as a hedge against the volatility of traditional equities. Rather than focusing on price growth, credit investments typically emphasize providing returns through income. However, FS Credit Opportunities (FSCO) differentiates itself with its history of providing both capital appreciation and income. I previously covered FSCO and issued a buy rating due to its strong earnings and healthy dividend coverage. Since the time of my last coverage, the macro environment has changed and the fund has released an updated report, so I wanted to revisit FSCO to see how its value proposition may have changed since then.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see how FSCO's price has increased by about 12.6%. The fund has even managed to rapidly recover from the market declined caused by President Trump's initial tariff proposals. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 26.3% over the same time frame. The main appeal of maintaining a position in FSCO is the generous starting dividend yield of about 11%. This double-digit yield in combination with the monthly payouts, makes this an enticing choice for income investors.

Despite a challenging macroeconomic environment, FSCO has continued to deliver strong results. This has caused the discount pricing to shrink over recent months. So while it may not be the best time to initiate a position from a traditional valuation sense, there are plenty of metrics that confirm FSCO can continue to provide value for shareholders. This is impressive when we consider that FSCO is still relatively new with an inception dating back to only 2022. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology that the fund utilizes to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most updated fund overview, FSCO has total assets under management of $2.14B that is spread across 77 different portfolio companies. The fund focuses on a diverse range of debt investments with a primary focus on the senior secured first lien structure, making up 72% of assets. This is the most ideal structure because senior secured first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This limits the overall risk of the investment for FSCO and reduces the probability that the fund loses all invested capital if a deal doesn't work out as anticipated.

The second-largest portion of FSCO's investments are structured as equity investments at 10% of assets. Equity investments sit at the bottom of the corporate capital structure and have the lowest priority for repayment, making these the riskiest form of investment. While equity investments may not produce the most consistent level of cash flows, they provide FSCO with the opportunity for capital appreciation when the valuation of the underlying borrower increases.

From a sector perspective, Consumer Services makes up the largest portion of the investments at 15%. This is followed by exposure to Healthcare Equipment and Commercial & Professional service investments, accounting for 12% and 11% of assets respectively. The combination of these sectors makes up 38% of total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on FSCO's daily price movements and total return potential.

Lastly, 69% of investments are structured on a floating rate basis. This has helped FSCO capitalize on this higher interest rate environment by essentially collecting more interest income from borrowers. The higher the interest rate, the higher the interest expense that borrowers are required to pay on the debt they hold on their balance sheet. The downside is that FSCO also utilizes leverage to help fuel portfolio growth and the debt has an effective cost of borrowing of 5.71%.

Valuation

Due to FSCO's strong performance over the last twelve months, FSCO's discount to NAV level has become considerably smaller. At the moment, FSCO now trades at a small discount to NAV of nearly 4%. For reference, FSCO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 17.2% over the last three-year period. Despite the discount levels shrinking, it may still be a solid time to initiate a long-term position. If the underlying performance, NAV growth, and earnings remain strong, it seems FSCO is quickly heading towards the premium territory within the next twelve months.

In an effort to get better clarity into the fund's performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report as of the end of 2024. Net investment income landed at $0.87 per share while net realized gains amounted to $0.07 per share. Looking at prior years, we can see how the level of net investment income has continued to rise over time, which reinforces the idea that FSCO has been able to efficiently capitalize on this higher interest rate environment and have been able to grow their base level of assets. What I really like about the fund is that net investment income has always outpaced the annual distribution paid.

This means that FSCO hasn't been reliant on net realized gains to fuel the growth of the fund. This is why FSCO is capable of preserving investor capital during market declines and has been able to keep a strong NAV consistency since its inception. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $7.30 per share
  • 2021: $7.64 per share
  • 2022: $6.33 per share
  • 2023: $6.92 per share
  • 2024: $7.15 per share

However, the latest quarterly report indicates that sales and repayments of $288M have outpaced new purchases of $163M. It seems like FSCO is remaining selective with its new investments during this period of market uncertainty linked to tariffs and interest rates. I'd prefer this selective approach because it ensures that only the highest quality borrowers become part of FSCO's portfolio.

As a result, we believe we have a solid understanding of the portfolio's first order exposure to tariffs. However, we remain cautious as second and third order effects are still uncertain and may take time to materialize depending on how the tariff situation evolves. Despite the sluggish M&A environment, we saw strong origination activity supported by an expansive sourcing network. - Nicholas Heilbut, Director of Research of FS Global Credit

Judging from the fund's consistent performance since its inception, I feel confident that FSCO will eventually trade in the premium to NAV territory. Even if the fund's net realized gains don't improve, I expect superior positive returns since the distribution is well-supported by net investment income. Looking at the performance since inception, we can see how FSCO's price has increased by more than 56.5% while the total return is more than 114%.

Risk Profile

Since a large majority of the fund operates on a floating rate basis, I believe there to be an increased risk during this period of elevated interest rates. While higher interest rates may be beneficial for net investment income levels, they can also put stress on borrowers that are underperforming. A higher cost of debt can be troublesome for borrowers that do not have consistent cash flows, which is why the higher interest rate environment can lead to an increase in defaults and non-accruals.

Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. When I previously covered FSCO, non-accruals amounted to 3% of fair portfolio value. The latest earnings call confirmed that non-accruals improved, but I struggled to find an exact figure through any of the released earnings documents. Surprisingly, non-accruals weren't talked about in detail on the latest earnings call.

We had some accretive exits during Q1. And then we also had some appreciation of certain investments, including our Lifescan Global and our Correct Care Solutions investments, two names that were previously on non-accrual, but performed very well during the quarter. - Andrew Beckman, Head of FS Global Credit

S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults can rise to 1.6% in a base case scenario, assuming slower economic growth and rising prices that create headwinds for borrowers. However, S&P Global estimates that leveraged loan defaults could increase as high as 3% if the political tension from tariffs escalates. We still don't know what the ripple effects of the tariff proposals will be, so I believe that the current market conditions will lead to defaults closer to the pessimistic scenario below.

Additionally, it doesn't seem like markets expect to see any interest rate cuts soon. The Fed has decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their last meeting as they continue to observe inflationary data. I believe that the rising costs of goods will ultimately lead to higher inflation. Since inflation already sits higher than the Fed's targeted level, I don't see a scenario where any sizable interest rate cuts will happen.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0645 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.774 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, the net investment income levels have now grown to outpace this annual payout amount. Just for reference, here are the net investment income amounts since the fund's inception:

  • 2020: $0.57 per share
  • 2021: $0.56 per share
  • 2022: $0.68 per share
  • 2023: $0.77 per share
  • 2024: $0.87 per share

Looking back over the fund's history, we can see that the distributions have been gradually increased since inception. The combination of strong covered from earnings and base distribution increases, reinforces that FSCO is a great option for investors seeking a source of reliable income. I only see the dividend being threatened in a scenario where interest rates are cut in the near term or if non-accruals increased. This would likely lead to lower net investment income levels at some point. However, there's a good chance that management can offset any decreases in net investment income by allocating capital towards new portfolio companies.

In addition, it's also worth mentioning that the distributions received from FSCO are typically classified as ordinary dividend income. This is expected since the distributions are funded by net investment income. Based on the amount of capital invested, your specific tax bracket, or desired income level, a position in FSCO would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on FSCO as the fund continues to demonstrate positive NAV growth and consistent growth in earnings. The fact that the fund's distributions are consistently covered by net investment income alone, takes away from the fund's reliance on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth. The strong cash generation has led to several base distribution raises since the fund's inception, making it a great choice for income investors. I do have some concerns that the higher interest rate environment can lead to more non-accruals, but the fund's consistency instills confidence that they'll be able to navigate challenging market conditions.

data.title: Manulife Financial: Asia Engine Drives Steady Growth
data.description:
Introduction

It's been 6 months since my last piece on Manulife Financial Corporation (NYSE:MFC) (TSX:MFC:CA). In November, I rated the stock as a 'buy' noting excellent acceleration in growth in Asia and the Wealth and Asset Management business, despite a few challenges in the North American market. With the company's latest Q1'25 results that were released on May 7, I think investors saw a continuation of these trends with management committed to returning excess cash to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. While the stock has fallen by 3% since November, I think the valuation remains attractive as ROE climbs higher, the balance sheet remains in great shape, and the company's focus on asset-light growth positions them for improving margins.

Strong Asia Sales Growth, Higher ECL Provision

Manulife's first quarter results were right in line with sellside estimates, as evidenced by a muted share price reaction following the quarter. In Q1'25, the company's core earnings came in at $1.45 billion which came in slightly better thanks to a $35 million pre-tax charge in the P&C business from California wildfires and a $46 million pre-tax ECL. This led to operating EPS of 99 cents (a penny above consensus), representing about 4% growth from Q1'24 after adjusting for these one-time items.

At a high level, Manulife's growth was helped by strong APE sales growth of 37% with particularly strong results in Asia and Canada with NBV growth of 36%, higher interest on allocated capital, and continued expense discipline in Global WAM. That resulted in margin outperformance with core EBITDA margins coming in at 28.4%. On the flip side, the company saw net adverse experience in Corporate from CA wildfire losses and in the US segment slightly, as well as higher provision for ECL which offset the positives.

When looking at the results by geography, in Asia, Manulife's operating income of $705 million was 12.6% higher than last year as a result of higher investment income on allocated capital and a moderately lower tax rate at around 12.5%. APE sales were pretty strong in my view as they grew 48.6% to $1.4 billion. Growth was materially stronger across all footprints, mostly notably in Hong Kong where APE sales grew 172% to $517 million driven by strong growth across savings, health, and protection products to both mainland Chinese visitor and domestic customers. NBV margin of 49.9% was a bit weak relative to the 63.7% posted in Q1 of last year due to overall product mix.

In Canada, Manulife benefitted from favorable insurance experience and growth in Group Insurance, partially offset by an increase in ECL provisions and lower Manulife Bank earnings. On the earnings call post the quarter, management highlighted that the recent reinsurance transaction reduced core earnings slightly. Altogether, operating income was up 2.7% year over year, with overall APE climbing 9.1%.

The U.S. was in my view the weaker part of the quarterly results. In the U.S. business, operating income came in at $361 million, down 20.1% year over year with a mix of unfavorable claims experience to the tune of $30 million, lower expected investment earnings at $373 million, at a larger ECL provision of $17 million.

Finally, in GWAM, operating income was strong at $454 million, up 30% with a core EBITDA margin of 28.4%. From an AUMA standpoint, net flows were positive at $489 million, but this was offset by a better than expected investment income loss which resulted in a higher AUMA balance at quarter end around $1.0 billion.

Manulife Has a Healthy Balance Sheet

Manulife's balance sheet is one of the best in the insurance business. The company's LICAT ratio clocked in at 137% at quarter end with a financial leverage ratio of 23.9%, comfortably below the 25% medium-term target. This tells me the company has good financial flexibility to pursue organic growth, return capital, or weather economic uncertainty. Indeed, on return of capital, the company repurchased over a half billion dollars worth of shares.

This is all the more impressive when we consider that book value has increased by 12% to $36.66. With another $700+ million also returned through dividends in the quarter, capital return to shareholders via dividends and buybacks amounted to $1.2 billion, which is roughly a distributable cash yield of 8.5% on a TTM basis relative to the company's $75.2 billion market cap.

Outlook

I've been bullish for a while on Manulife and I haven't seen much of anything to suggest that sentiment should change. When we look at the outlook ahead for the company, I feel pretty confident that Manulife's Asia business should continue to perform well. With 50% APE sales growth this quarter, there's still a lot of momentum here and the resilience of the Global WAM's positive net flows signal to me that Manulife should have a ton of growth ahead of them.

I've long felt that insurers like Manulife don't deserve to trade at a discount to Canadian banks and because of a gradual shift into wealth management, the organic growth profiles of these businesses have improved. Indeed, even the company's ROEs in the 16-17% range have upside to move towards 18% and beyond. In high-potential markets like Hong Kong, Singapore, and mainland China, there's an underserved market for Manulife to tap.

Regarding guidance, management reiterated its medium-term earnings growth target of 10% to 12% with Asia expected to contribute significantly, though no specific segment-level guidance was provided for 2025 or 2026. To me, this suggests a steady-state expectation for continued organic growth, particularly from Asia's strong CSM growth (12% normalized organic growth in Q1'25) and Global WAM's consistent inflows. This, I think, will pave the way for more capital returns to shareholders to support share buybacks and dividends too.

Looking longer term, I'm encouraged by Manulife's strategic de-risking, with legacy businesses like long-term care and variable annuities now contributing less than 10% of core earnings. In conjunction with reduced ALDA exposure (down to 7% of invested assets), I feel the company is positioned quite well to weather economic cycles while focusing on higher-return, lower-risk segments, but I'll still be watching for any signs of deferred consumer purchasing decisions amid trade uncertainty. The reaffirmed 2027 targets, including expense efficiency and capital deployment, reinforce my view that Manulife is focused on sustainable growth and shareholder value creation.

Valuation

On valuation, Manulife trades in line with the rest of the peer group. At 10.3x earnings, the company trades at 1.7x book, which is around the level of most U.S. peers but below Canadian peer Sun Life Financial (SLF:CA) at 11.6x P/E (source: Bloomberg).

When we consider that Manulife's growth rates have accelerated and can reasonably grow its EPS at an 8-10% rate over the medium term, I don't feel that investors are overpaying in the slightest for a company like Manulife. The company has got great structural tailwinds ahead of it and strong growth in wealth management and Asia is poised to continue.

As for the risks to the investment thesis, The potential for trade tensions and market volatility to impact consumer sentiment is something that should be watched carefully by potential investors of Manulife as recent headline risk introduces a bit of uncertainty in the near term. That said, I believe the company's focus on digital transformation and customer experience (evidenced by the remarkable Net Promoter Score improvement in Asia), will continue to drive demand for protection and savings products even if short waterfall effects from tariffs could temporarily dampen sales volumes. Manulife's got a great balance sheet to weather any potential headwinds, so that gives me enough confidence that they should be able to meet any short-term challenges.

In addition to this risk, one other risk I'm thinking more about revolves around competitive pressures. In Asia, heightened competition (especially in high-growth markets like Hong Kong and Singapore) could erode margins if Manulife is forced to adjust pricing or product offerings to maintain market share, especially as broker channels, which grew significantly in Q1'25. I think the first signs of this would be a slowing growth rate followed by a plateau or drop in ROE, but until we see greater signs of this, I think Manulife has a strong foothold in these markets.

Takeaway

In conclusion, Manulife continues to demonstrate solid performance, particularly through its high-growth Asia segment and strong results in Global Wealth and Asset Management, despite some headwinds in the U.S. and headwinds that we saw related to credit provisions this quarter. The company's strong balance sheet and a disciplined capital return strategy is something I continue to view favorably. The valuation remains attractive, especially when considering the firm's ROE trajectory and medium-term earnings growth potential. While risks such as trade uncertainty and competitive pressures in Asia remain on the radar, Manulife's strategic positioning, capital flexibility, and ongoing shift toward asset-light, higher-margin businesses leave me confident in its long-term investment case.

data.title: CareTrust REIT: Don't Let The Earnings Miss Overshadow This Emerging Superstar
data.description:
Introduction

CareTrust REIT (NYSE:CTRE) is a company I've been bullish on for a few years now. Despite the challenging economic environment, CTRE has continued to focus on growth, setting them apart from peers alike. Moreover, they are a healthcare REIT, meaning they look poised to capture growth from aging baby boomers.

While the FED's decision surrounding interest rates remains uncertain due to potential stagflation, investors should look to accumulate shares in CareTrust REIT before the investment world catches on.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why CareTrust REIT looks poised to continue their strong growth trajectory for the foreseeable future.

Previous Thesis

I last covered CareTrust REIT back in February in an article titled: Expected Double-Digit Growth And Recent Pullback Make This Stock A Long-Term Buy.

CTRE closed out 2024 strong with $1.5 billion worth of investments, higher than 2023 that saw nearly $1 billion.

Since then, the REIT has significantly outperformed the S&P (SP500) at the time of writing, up roughly 17% compared to the index, down closer to 1%. During the third quarter, FFO was in-line with analysts' estimates at $0.40 while revenue also beat estimates.

Their Agree Realty (ADC) like balance sheet and end of 2026 price target of roughly $38 a share also made CTRE highly attractive.

With one of the lowest net debt-to-EBITDAs I've ever seen at just 0.5x and no debt maturing until 2027, I assumed CTRE would see a rating upgrade in the near future.

Double-Digit Growth Despite Miss

CareTrust REIT reported their Q1 earnings earlier this month and managed to miss on both their top and bottom lines. FFO of $0.42 was slightly below the $0.43 consensus while revenue missed by $2.23 million, coming in at $96.62 million.

The earnings miss was likely due to a misjudgment by analysts. However, CTRE's year-over-year growth was impressive as both their top and bottom lines grew an impressive 53.36% and 67.4% respectively.

Revenue grew year-over-year from $63 million while FFO grew from $44.9 million to $77.5 million. EBITDA also saw strong growth from $56.9 million to $87.6 million.

On a per-share basis, FFO growth was still impressive at 20%, likely due to the REIT's strong focus on growth. For comparison, peer American Healthcare REIT (AHR) saw higher FFO growth of 26.7% from $0.30 to $0.38.

During the quarter CareTrust REIT made 3 investments for $47 million at a double-digit yield of 10%.

These included a mezzanine loan, a skilled nursing facility, and a senior housing facility. This brought their total portfolio to 408 properties across 34 states.

As seen by CTRE's size and market cap of $5.59 billion, the REIT has plenty of room to see continuous growth in the foreseeable future. And as they continue to expand their portfolio, I expect their share price to follow.

Expanding Outside The U.S.

Earlier this month, CareTrust REIT closed on their $856 million acquisition of Care REIT plc, entering into the United Kingdom. The acquisition added 132 care homes and strengthened CTRE's portfolio, adding an additional $68.6 million in annual revenue.

As a result, CTRE's management raised their 2025 guidance. FFO is now expected in a range of $1.69 - $1.73, up from $1.68 - $1.72. Funds available for distribution, similar to AFFO, is expected to be between $1.73 - $1.77, up from $1.72 - $1.76 previously.

Moreover, as we gain more clarity on the economy and interest rates fall, I wouldn't be surprised to see management raise guidance further, resulting in potential price appreciation.

Balance Sheet Upgrade

During my last article I touched on how I believed CareTrust REIT could receive an upgrade to its credit rating any day as a result of their low leverage, well-staggered debt, and growth. They also had ample liquidity with $45 million cash on hand and $825 million revolver capacity.

Back in December, they received an upgrade from S&P to BB+, a notch below investment-grade. The agency upgraded its issue-level rating on CTRE's unsecured notes to an investment-grade rating of BBB-, which is a move in the right direction.

Moreover, with CTRE's low leverage of just 0.5x, and well-staggered debt with none maturing for the next three years, I can see another potential upgrade from one or all of the 3 major agencies over the next 1 to 2 years, giving the REIT cheaper access to capital, opening up additional growth opportunities for the foreseeable future.

Dividend Safety

Speaking of CareTrust REIT's double-digit top and bottom-line growth, they also raised their dividend double-digits from $0.29 to $0.335 this past March.

Using their dividend estimate of $1.26, this gives CTRE a very safe FAD payout ratio of 72% using their guidance midpoint of $1.75.

This also gives them room to conduct double-digit dividend increases in the future as well as an ample amount of retained capital to grow organically.

$500 Million Pipeline

CareTrust REIT's management stated back in December that they were on track to see similar investments to last year with approximately $1.5 million projected this year.

Their pipeline remained strong at approximately $500 million, which primarily consists of skilled nursing facilities as well as senior housing facilities.

Upside Potential

While CareTrust REIT's forward multiple of 16.78x is higher than the sector median and peer average of 14.86x, their strong growth, balance sheet, and fundamentals warrant a higher premium.

Here's how they compared to some of their healthcare peers in terms of valuation:

American Healthcare REIT: 22.0x

Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA): 12.02x

National Health Investors (NHI): 15.94x

Healthcare Realty Trust (HR): 9.51x

While near-term upside may be limited due to uncertainty, I think CareTrust REIT could see strong upside over the next 1 to 2 years, particularly if interest rates are much lower, which I expect them to be. If so, I see CTRE moving closer to their high price target and possibly even surpassing it.

Risk

A risk for CTRE is REITs' sensitivity to interest rates. Another risk is most of their portfolio is concentrated in California, a state known for controversy. This is often a turn off for investors as the state is subject to stricter laws, higher taxes, and natural disasters like earthquakes & wildfires.

Long-term, I expect CareTrust REIT to seek investments outside the state of California due to its known controversy. But going forward, their California concentration as well as uncertainty surrounding interest rates could limit upside and even result in potential downside.

Bottom Line

CareTrust REIT's latest quarter showing double-digit FFO, FAD, & revenue growth is a testament to their management team. Moreover, as interest rates are likely to fall further over the next 6 to 12 months, investors are likely to see strong upside.

Either way, I expect management to continue operating as usual with a strong focus on acquisitions, expanding the portfolio outside of California and possibly the U.S. in the near future.

Fundamentally, CTRE remains one of the best healthcare-focused REITs and their recent acquisitions over $1 billion is a further testament of their future growth potential. As a result of this and their long-term upside potential, I continue to rate CareTrust REIT a buy.

data.title: New Home Sales Showing Signs Of Reversing Downtrend
data.description:
The total market capitalization of new homes sold in the United States dipped slightly in April 2025. Political Calculations' initial estimate of the time-shifted trailing twelve-month average of the total value of new homes sold during the month is $28.22 billion.

This figure is down just 0.4% from our revised estimate of $28.36 billion in new homes sales in March 2025. The March 2025 data was revised upward from the initial estimate of $27.87 billion we reported last month.

The following charts present the U.S. new home market capitalization, the number of new home sales, and their sale prices as measured by their time-shifted, trailing twelve-month averages from January 1976 through April 2025. The trends they show suggest the new home market is starting to reverse its general downtrend since July 2024.

Flattening new home sales trend:

Flattening trend for home prices:

The transition from a falling to a flat trendline in the trailing twelve-month averages comes as the reported number of new home sales surged in April 2025. Seeking Alpha summarized April 2025's underlying monthly data that fed into our overall estimate of the market cap for new homes:

U.S. new home sales jumped 10.9% M/M to 743K in April, rising from 724K in March and surging past the 700K consensus estimate, according to data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Friday. The increased sales rate led to an increase in the median sales price and a reduction in inventory.

On a Y/Y basis, new home sales rose 3.3%.

The median sales price of new houses was $407.2K, up 0.8% from March's $403.7K and below April 2024's price of $415.3K. The average sales price of new houses, though, was $518.4K, 3.7% higher than the previous month and 3.6% above April 2024's level.

If the numbers hold, April 2025's new home sales will be the highest recorded since February 2022. New home sales data is subject to frequent revisions before being finalized, where the size of revisions can be significant.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 23 May 2025.

U.S. Census Bureau. New Residential Sales Historical Data. Median and Average Sale Price of Houses Sold. [Excel Spreadsheet]. Accessed 23 May 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KOLD Can Be Leveraged During Short-Term Volatility
data.description:
ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Natural Gas (KOLD) is a leveraged inverse ETF designed to provide traders 2x the inverse daily performance of the Bloomberg Natural Gas Subindex. The fund directly invests in natural gas futures, placing a short investment on the front month of natural gas and rolls forward the contract date upon expiration. Given my long-term bullish expectations for natural gas, I believe KOLD should be used solely for day trading; I am recommending KOLD with a HOLD rating.

Unlike leveraged stock portfolios, KOLD solely trades futures contracts, providing full exposure to natural gas.

The portfolio is designed to provide 2x the daily inverse returns of the underlying natural gas index, suggesting that traders that participate in a position are short natural gas. It should be emphasized that the strategy was designed to be traded on a daily basis in order to achieve the 2x return target. Holding the strategy for longer than a single day may lead to significant deviation from the 2x target, creating a compounding effect given the daily reset of the underlying derivatives. Given the high volatility in natural gas trading, this can create significant challenges for long-term investors, particularly if the underlying natural gas prices were to increase across consecutive trading days.

Given the comparison between the long-term returns vs. the daily returns, it should be clear that holding this ETF for longer than a day may provide unwanted, compounding exposure.

In certain instances, the equities market can potentially be a leading indicator for large moves in KOLD. Using Expand Energy (EXE) and EQT Corporation (EQT) as examples, shares oftentimes react to initial declines in the gas market, frontrunning the larger gas price decline. Though there are only a handful of instances of looking at the pricing charts from a high, long-term level, this could potentially add to one’s trading strategy.

Looking at the natural gas strip price, holding KOLD beyond a single day may not be an ideal strategy. Futures are priced for a relatively bullish gas market going into the end of 2025, potentially leading to relatively lackluster returns if holding KOLD beyond a single day. From a day-to-day trading perspective, KOLD could potentially add to a trader’s strategy, particularly as uncertainty remains in the market as trade tariff policy is determined.

It was recently announced that the US Court of International Trade struck down the broad stroke tariff policy imposed by President Trump. This follows Liberation Day, which initiated tariff policy on April 2, 2025, before reducing virtually all trade tariffs to 10% during trade negotiations. Overall, events like these add volatility and uncertainty into the market, creating trading opportunities for those seeking to add short-term risk to their strategies.

From a macroeconomic perspective, I believe expected demand for natural gas and production will drive the market. Domestically, shale production, particularly in the Permian Basin, may dictate a significant proportion of gas production. The gas-to-oil ratio [GOR] in the Permian Basin currently sits at roughly 40%.

For a large international integrated oil company [IOC] like Exxon Mobil (XOM), I believe that the Permian Basin can serve as an ancillary production facility as shale production is relatively short-cycled when compared to offshore production. This means that production in the Permian Basin could potentially hold a certain degree of variability when oil prices are in decline. Lower production in the basin should translate to less gas being produced in the basin, creating a tighter gas market and potentially leading to gas price appreciation.

The same can be expected with natural gas in which during periods of higher prices, dry gas producers may seek to produce more to take advantage of the more appealing price point.

There are two sides to the coin; on the one hand, EQT Corporation previously guided pulling back on gas production in order to maintain its high-quality asset book, waiting for prices to appreciate with increased domestic offtake. Expand Energy, on the other hand, has targeted higher production volumes for 2025 as LNG export capacity comes online. Though higher production oftentimes cures high prices for natural gas, I believe that with the increase in offtake to support increased production, gas prices shouldn’t be negatively impacted.

One trading factor that may strike as appealing is the potential correlation between EQT shares and KOLD. Management at EQT recently indicated that they will be allowing for their hedge book to roll off without replacing hedges for 2026 given their bullish outlook for the natural gas market. Given that EQT may be moving into an environment with less protection against gas price swings, shares may become more reflective of the volatility embedded in the natural gas market, holding all else equal. Now, this may not hold depending on EQT’s potential offtake agreements for direct sales to natural gas-fired power plants in support of the growing demand by the hyperscalers for load capacity. Depending on how offtake contracts are designed, EQT may very well be just as, if not more, hedged than before.

With this notion in mind, if offtake agreements are written between gas producers and the hyperscalers with an embedded price floor, one has to wonder what this will do to the broader domestic gas market and whether it will create more stability in daily pricing. With the presumption that gas producers remain disciplined and produce to the same level as offtake capacity, will gas price swings be as dramatic as previously experienced during a macroeconomic anomaly?

Conclusion

Overall, KOLD is a leveraged natural gas trading strategy that targets 2x the inverse daily returns of the Bloomberg Natural Gas Subindex. For day-to-day trading, KOLD could be an appealing vehicle for active traders seeking to amplify the price swings. For a long-term strategy, I do not believe KOLD would be ideal given my bullish outlook on the gas market. I am recommending KOLD with a HOLD rating.

data.title: Microsoft: More Resilient To Trade Wars Than Peers
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It has been a while since I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT). My previous bullish article on MSFT was published in early August, and the stock's total return slightly outpaced the broader market's growth since then. My stance remains bullish due to Microsoft's intact strategic positioning, which is fortified by the robust ongoing secular digitalization trend. In addition, the stock remains very attractively valued. All in all, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for MSFT.

Recent Developments

The company's latest quarterly earnings were released on April 30, accompanied by a robust double beat against consensus forecasts. A big bullish indication is the fact that MSFT's FQ3 2025 YoY revenue growth accelerated to 13.3%, compared to the 12.3% growth delivered in FQ2 2025.

The impressive YoY adjusted EPS expansion is also another vital bullish indicator. Microsoft's non-GAAP EPS achieved a record level of $3.46 in FQ3 2025, which is significantly higher compared to $2.94 generated during the same quarter of FY2024.

The quality of the EPS expansion always matters a lot to me because this is the primary indication of the company's ability to demonstrate operating leverage through cross-selling and the economies of scale effect. Despite MSFT's vast scale, the company continues delivering operating margin expansion. The YoY increase from 44.6% to 45.7% looks very impressive. As a result, Microsoft's free cash flow [FCF] generating capacity remains intact. This factor supports the company's fortress financial position, providing more opportunities to finance growth projects. The last but not least significant bullish indicator from the latest earnings release is that MSFT demonstrated confident growth across all segments, which indicates an extremely strong business mix.

I would like to remind readers about Microsoft's unique positioning in the AI revolution, as it has a very close strategic partnership with OpenAI. OpenAI owns ChatGPT, the world's by far most popular chatbot that commands almost a 60% market share. In addition, Microsoft's own chatbot solution, Copilot, is also among the largest players by the market share. Having such a solid exposure to the thriving generative AI field combined with Microsoft's massive 21% market share in cloud is a solid blend to maximize the company's cross-selling potential.

The upcoming earnings release is scheduled for late July, and the setup looks bullish. First, the company's FQ4 2025 YoY revenue growth is expected to accelerate again to around 14%. Second, the adjusted EPS is expected to slightly outpace revenue growth, which once again proves MSFT's massive operating leverage. Third, there were significantly more upward EPS revisions than downgrades over the last 90 days, indicating extremely strong Wall Street sentiment regarding MSFT's ability to deliver strong earnings.

My optimism is also backed by several bullish outlook reiterations from prominent Wall Street analysts during May. Several largest firms [like Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley] remain bullish on Microsoft's future prospects, which is always a positive sign for me. On average, Wall Street analysts believe that MSFT's fair share price is above $500.

Finally, I think that Microsoft is the best positioned in the current uncertain environment caused by global trade tensions if we are speaking about the world's three largest tech companies. The fact that Microsoft's software-centric business model ["Windows and Devices" sales represented only around 6% of the total revenue during the nine months ended March 31, 2025] is not significantly dependent on imports and exports is a great advantage in the current environment. For example, we see that two other giants, Apple or Nvidia, face quite notable challenges in the current U.S.-China trade war because their business models are much more dependent on selling physical products. This gives MSFT investors another crucial advantage and protection against global trade war risks.

Valuation Update

The discounted cash flow [DCF] model is my favorite tool to value large growth stocks because long-term revenue projections are usually quite reliable, and there is much more predictability in the future earnings growth trajectory. The DCF approach starts with figuring out the discount rate, which is the company's cost of equity due to the low reliance on debt. The CAPM approach suggests that the discount rate is 8.73%, which I consider reasonable.

The first-year FCF margin assumption is always conservative in all my DCF models because future growth is not guaranteed [despite being highly probable]. Therefore, I start with calculating the ex-stock-based compensation [ex-SBC] margin. According to the TTM metrics, MSFT's ex-SBC FCF margin is 16%. This is the year-one assumption, expected to demonstrate growth by 100-bps per year due to MSFT's historically strong operating leverage.

Overall, the long-term revenue growth trajectory suggested by consensus is reasonable, as on average, it incorporates revenue growth rate deceleration over time. Therefore, for MSFT's DCF model, I relied on consensus estimates. As a result, DCF's revenue CAGR until FY2032 is around 11%, which looks conservative enough for the tech behemoth like MSFT. A 5% growth rate was applied to MSFT's termination value due to the company's strong positioning to drive both organic [acquiring new customers and cross-selling potential] and inorganic [historically strong M&A record] growth over the long term.

The business's fair value is close to $4 trillion, according to my DCF model. With the current market cap of circa $3.4 trillion, MSFT is 17% undervalued. In the second scenario, I want to demonstrate that the market currently assumes a 4.3% perpetual growth rate, which is unfairly conservative for a company like Microsoft. I think that this is unfair due to the double-digit revenue CAGR observed over the last decade, plus the projected aggressive EPS and revenue growth for the next ten years.

To wrap up, I want to outline the target price calculation in the final valuation working. Multiplying the current share price of around $460 by 1.17 [which incorporates the aforementioned 17% upside potential] gives me the target price of $538.

Risks Update

Competition in the cloud industry remains fierce as the race involves other giants like Google and Amazon. All three cloud giants demonstrate aggressive revenue growth rates, which clearly indicate that the competition is poised to remain intense. Moreover, all three behemoths are approximately equally aggressive in their AI infrastructure investments. This means that the balance sheet strength is crucial because data centers require multi-billion investments and Microsoft looks notably weaker than Google from this perspective. Please do not get me wrong here, Microsoft's financial position is a fortress with almost $80 billion in cash and quite low leverage. But Google's financial position is even stronger.

In addition, I think we all remember last year's sharp MSFT sell-off, which was triggered by the global outage in Microsoft's services. Despite the fact that this outage was not Microsoft's fault but due to unforeseen issues during CrowdStrike's (CRWD) software update, the situation significantly damaged Microsoft's reputation. Microsoft's dominance is not only the company's large asset but also means extremely high expectations regarding the reliability of its services. Thus, there are substantial risks related to potential outages of Microsoft's services, even if they are caused by external partners.

According to last decade's by-month seasonality chart, June has been historically one of the weakest months for MSFT, with just a 50% win rate. Therefore, investors might see some share price weakness next month. On the other hand, July is historically strong, which is highly likely related to the calendar Q2 earnings release. Therefore, dollar-cost averaging might be a prudent option to further accumulate the MSFT position.

Bottom Line

To conclude, MSFT is still a "Strong Buy". The current share price of around $460 looks like a great buying opportunity, given a substantial upside potential suggested by my valuation update. Last but not least, Microsoft's fundamentals remain intact amid the current thriving digital environment.
